Add some information relevant to the pricing of images with Nano Banana as that is the model we are integrated with. Here is some google search data to use, add more if you have it : Nano Banana (Google's Gemini image model) costs vary by tier, with free use for basic Gemini app users (limited), around $0.039 for standard 1K/2K API images, and up to $0.13 - $0.24 per image for 4K/higher quality via API, depending on the provider and exact tokens used. Standard API costs are around $0.039/image (1290 tokens), while Pro API can reach $0.134 for 1K/2K or $0.24 for 4K, often bundled into subscriptions or pay-per-token models. 
Cost Breakdown by Model & Tier:
Free Tier (Gemini App): Limited low-resolution images at no direct cost. 
Nano Banana (Basic API): Around $0.039 per image for 1024x1024 resolution (1290 tokens). 
Nano Banana Pro (API/Developer):
1K/2K Resolution: ~$0.134 - $0.14 per image (1120 tokens). 
4K Resolution: ~$0.24 per image (2000 tokens). 
Subscription (e.g., Gemini Advanced): May offer access to Pro features without per-image charges, but often with resolution limits. 
Factors Influencing Cost:
Resolution: Higher resolutions (like 4K) cost significantly more due to increased token usage. 
Tokens: Pricing is token-based, so complex prompts or high-res images use more tokens, increasing cost. 
Platform: Costs can differ slightly between direct Google API, third-party platforms (like WaveSpeedAI), or integrated services (like Cursor).

To get the conversation id, we simply need to find a conversation, click the button to open the conversation popup window, search for the where xxxx is the item id. Then we need to click the button to close the conversation popup :

View buyer

We need to get the filename of the photo url without the extension. we will return that as a fileNameId value.

When we are syncing the tracked properties, we need to also get the listingId for all the published (active/pending/sold) listings. We can find those listingIds in the JSON contents of script tags on the page. The scripts all have the form <script type="application/json" data-content-len="<some_number_goes_here>" data-sjs data-processed="1"> The content of this script is a well formed json object. That json object will have a subkey somewhere nested deep of 'marketplace_product_details_page" which contains an object "target". the target object has an "id" key which is the listingId we need. it also has a listing_photo array where the 1st item is the first photo for the item. If we extract that filename and compare it to all the thumbnail photos for the items we can match the photo of the listing to the marketplace listing id. That is how we return a listingId. We will also return the fileNameId which is the filename of the thumbnail without the extension. Let's update the scripts and the code and the service worker to support this.
The inbox should only show tracked listings that have a listingId in the marketplace.
